name: Project Soli
short_description: A miniature 60 GHz radar chip from Google ATAP that senses fine touchless hand micro-gestures and human presence.
long_description: Soli is a millimeter-wave frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar sensor and antenna array shrunk onto a single chip a few millimeters across. Announced at Google I/O 2015 and led by Ivan Poupyrev, it detects sub-millimeter hand motions at very high frame rates without cameras, light, or contact, and works through materials. It moved from a developer kit into consumer products: 'Motion Sense' touchless gestures and presence detection in the 2019 Pixel 4, and contactless 'Sleep Sensing' (breathing and movement tracking) in the 2nd-gen Nest Hub in 2021. As physical AI, Soli is significant as ambient, infrastructure-grade sensing: the device perceives gesture and presence while staying invisible, raising it as a privacy-friendlier alternative to cameras for in-the-environment interaction.

images.caption: Front schematic of the Google Pixel 4, the first consumer phone to ship the Soli radar (Motion Sense) behind the top bezel.
images.alt_text: A line schematic of the front of a Google Pixel 4 smartphone, with a wide top bezel that houses the Soli radar sensor used for Motion Sense.

sources.title: Soli: Ubiquitous Gesture Sensing with Millimeter Wave Radar
sources.publisher: ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH), Vol. 35, No. 4
sources.access_notes: Authors: Jaime Lien, Nicholas Gillian, M. Emre Karagozler, Patrick Amihood, Carsten Schwesig, Erik Olson, Hakim Raja, Ivan Poupyrev (Google ATAP). Primary technical reference for the Soli FMCW radar approach.

sources.title: Soli: Radar-Based Perception and Interaction in Pixel 4
sources.access_notes: Describes 60 GHz FMCW radar, Motion Sense and presence detection in Pixel 4.

sources.title: Google's Soli radar returns to track sleep on the new Nest Hub
sources.access_notes: Coverage of Soli-powered contactless Sleep Sensing in the 2nd-gen Nest Hub.

sources.title: Google ATAP hopes its Project Soli and Project Jacquard will change how we interact with wearables
sources.access_notes: Documents the Google I/O 2015 announcement by ATAP.

interactions.title: Wave to skip a song
interactions.description: Swipe a hand left or right above the Pixel 4 to skip forward or back a track — no screen contact, and it works even with the display off or the music app closed.

interactions.title: Silence a ringing phone with one swipe
interactions.description: Wave once across the screen to snooze an alarm, quiet a timer, or mute an incoming call without grabbing the phone.

interactions.title: The phone quiets as you reach for it
interactions.description: Soli senses a hand approaching and lights up notifications and the clock before you touch it — and a ringing call drops to a softer volume the moment you reach toward the phone.

interactions.title: Pause music from across the bedroom
interactions.description: Lying in bed with your eyes closed, an imprecise air-tap over the 2nd-gen Nest Hub — accurate from roughly 18 inches away — stops the music, and a wave snoozes the morning alarm.

interactions.title: Track your breathing all night with no camera
interactions.description: Set on the nightstand a foot or two away, the Nest Hub's radar reads chest movement and breathing to chart your sleep, coughs, and snores through the dark without ever taking a picture.

interactions.title: Pokemon that wave back
interactions.description: A Pixel 4 demo, Pokemon Wave Hello, turned the radar into play: wave at the screen and the Pokemon waves, reacts, and lets you pet it with a gesture.

interactions.title: Telling steel from glass by radar alone
interactions.description: Researchers at St Andrews repurposed the Soli chip into RadarCat, training it to recognize materials and objects from their radar return — distinguishing metals, transparent liquids, and even individual body parts placed against the sensor.

interactions.title: A counter that knows when you've turned away
interactions.description: Mounted in a kitchen, the radar reads orientation as well as presence: a recipe display could hold its place when you turn to grab an ingredient and resume the moment you face it again — grounded in ATAP's 'Fields' proxemic sensing, not just on/off presence.
